StormOutlineSection.description: A section in the STORM-generated outline.

search_keywords.description: Comma-separated domain-specific keywords for routing evidence to this section. Include technical terms, units, method names. E.g. 'epoxy, MPa, dolly, ASTM D4541, cohesive failure'
search_keywords.title: Search Keywords

evidence_summary.description: Summary of evidence from conversations for this section
evidence_summary.title: Evidence Summary

perspectives.description: STORM perspectives that contributed to this section
perspectives.title: Perspectives

sections.description: Ordered sections for the research report

schema_json.title: StormOutlinePlan

prompt:
You are a research outline architect. Based on multi-perspective expert interviews, create a comprehensive report outline.

RESEARCH QUESTION: What are the proven health benefits and risks of intermittent fasting?

INTERVIEW CONVERSATIONS (5 perspectives, 5 total Q&A rounds):

=== Scientific (Dr. Hiroshi Tanaka) ===
  Q: In randomized controlled trials of 16:8 time-restricted eating in humans, what are the documented dose-response effects on HOMA-IR, fasting glucose, and beta-hydroxybutyrate thresholds, and how do the
  A: The search results only partially address Dr. Tanaka's highly specific question. For HOMA-IR and fasting glucose in 16:8 time-restricted eating (TRE), Source 1 reports an overall improvement in fasting glucose, HOMA-IR, insulin, and HDL-C in participants following 16/8 TRE. Source 9 quantifies this, noting a significant reduction in fasting blood glucose of approximately -3.69 units versus usual diets. However, Source 5 counters that fasting glucose did not change across 2-12 month TRE trials in
  Key findings: 16:8 TRE improves HOMA-IR, fasting insulin, and fasting glucose per Source 1, with Source 9 reportin; Timing matters: early TRF improves insulin sensitivity (Source 2, 3) while mid-day TRF does not; Sou; AMPK-MKO mice exhibit fasting-induced hyperketosis (Source 10), indicating AMPK gates ketone homeost; Intermittent fasting regulates the mTOR-autophagy axis in skeletal muscle (Source 12); sustained mTO; No retrieved source reports human 16:8 dose-response data for beta-hydroxybutyrate thresholds or qua

=== Regulatory (Maria Escalante, JD) ===
  Q: Under current FDA DSHEA boundaries and Codex Alimentarius labeling standards, what specific evidentiary thresholds and vulnerable-population warnings are required to distinguish lawful intermittent fa
  A: From a U.S. regulatory standpoint under DSHEA, the dividing line between lawful intermittent-fasting guidance and an unlawful disease claim turns on the type of claim made on meal-plan apps and nutraceutical labels. Under section 403(r)(6), a structure/function claim may only describe the role of a nutrient or dietary ingredient in affecting the normal structure or function of the human body [Source 1, Source 4]. DSHEA amended the FD&C Act to establish this framework and distinguish dietary supp
  Key findings: DSHEA section 403(r)(6) permits structure/function claims describing effects on normal body structur; The structure/function vs. disease claim determination is fact-specific, governed by FDA guidance do; The mandatory DSHEA disclaimer (not evaluated by FDA; not intended to diagnose/treat/cure/prevent di; Disease-related health claims require either 'significant scientific agreement' (authorized) or cred; Search results contain no Codex Alimentarius material, no intermittent-fasting-specific thresholds, 

=== Industry (Rajiv Menon, MBA) ===
  Q: What do 12-week retention curves and protocol-compliance telemetry from commercial intermittent fasting apps reveal about the real-world gap between RCT-reported metabolic benefits (weight loss, HbA1c
  A: From an industry telemetry standpoint, the available evidence reveals a substantial efficacy-effectiveness gap between controlled IF trials and at-home app usage. Commercial retention curves are stark: Source 3 (Tsai, 60,164 enrollees) reports 73% retention at 4 weeks collapsing to 42% at 13 weeks, 22% at 26 weeks, and only 6.6% at one year. Source 7's systematic review of major commercial programs corroborates this, finding 12-week attrition between 25% and 65% depending on program. Source 2 ex
  Key findings: Commercial weight-loss program retention drops from 73% at 4 weeks to 42% at 13 weeks and 6.6% at 52; IF mobile-app telemetry shows severe responder heterogeneity: BMI >=40 users lost 13.9% body weight ; RCT evidence shows IF is equivalent, not superior, to continuous energy restriction for weight loss ; 16/8 time-restricted eating improves fasting glucose, HOMA-IR, insulin, and HDL-C (Source 13), and e; The supplied sources contain no data on continuous glucose monitor integration with IF apps; this su

=== Economic (Dr. Eleanor Whitfield) ===
  Q: What is the incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) of intermittent fasting versus metformin and GLP-1 agonists for type 2 diabetes prevention, measured in cost per QALY gained over a 10-year hori
  A: The search results do not provide a direct head-to-head incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) of intermittent fasting versus metformin and GLP-1 receptor agonists for type 2 diabetes prevention over a 10-year horizon. No source reports a three-way comparison with cost-per-QALY estimates for this specific combination. However, adjacent evidence offers partial insight. Source 2 (Habka 2025) evaluated a fasting-mimicking diet (FMD), the closest analog to intermittent fasting in the results, a
  Key findings: No source provides a direct three-way ICER comparing intermittent fasting, metformin, and GLP-1 agon; Fasting-mimicking diet reduced diabetes complications by 11.4% and medication use by 67.2% versus us; Median ICER for diet and physical activity programs is $13,761/QALY from a health care perspective (; Lifestyle intervention averted 9.53% of T2DM cases, yielding 0.52 additional QALYs at net cost savin; GLP-1 agonists' cardiovascular benefits could improve their cost-effectiveness profile, but no preve

=== Public_Health (Dr. Amara Okonkwo, MPH) ===
  Q: How do the documented health benefits and risks of intermittent fasting vary across socioeconomic strata, and what evidence exists on its feasibility and safety when implemented through federal nutrit
  A: From a public health perspective, the evidence directly addressing how intermittent fasting (IF) benefits and risks vary across socioeconomic strata, or its integration into SNAP/WIC, is sparse and indirect. The umbrella review by Patikorn et al. [Sources 1, 2, 8] indicates IF may yield beneficial effects across a range of health outcomes for adults with overweight or obesity compared to caloric restriction, and a JACC meta-analysis [Source 3] reports slightly greater weight loss and waist-circu
  Key findings: IF produces modest weight, lipid, and glycemic benefits versus continuous caloric restriction in adu; Documented risks include mild nausea, dizziness, constipation (2-7%) and a signal of elevated cardio; Only one study directly examines IF in low-income populations (time-restricted feeding in low-income; Socioeconomic status is an established social determinant of diet-related disease, but no reviewed s; No evidence exists on IF implementation, feasibility, or safety within SNAP or WIC; federal nutritio

PERSPECTIVES COVERED: Industry, Public_Health, Economic, Scientific, Regulatory

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. Identify 8-15 thematic clusters across ALL conversations.
2. For each cluster, create a report section with:
   - title: Clear, descriptive section heading
   - description: 2-3 sentences on what this section should cover
   - evidence_summary: Key findings from conversations that belong here
   - perspectives: Which STORM perspectives contributed to this section
   - order: Sequential position (1-based)
3. Sections should be ordered logically: background -> methodology -> findings -> analysis -> implications -> future directions.
4. Each section should draw from at least 2 different perspectives where possible (multi-perspective synthesis).
5. Do NOT create a section for every single Q&A pair — cluster related information together.
6. Ensure COMPLETE COVERAGE: every key finding from the interviews should appear in at least one section's evidence_summary.

Return a JSON object with a 'sections' array of outline section objects.
